rows.item: 学習意欲・授業参加度

summary: The aim of this course is to introduce participants to the word of movies, focusing on recent releases from English speaking countries, their analysis and interpretation. We will partly watch and try to understand the narrative of a series of famous movies. A plot summary and a number of questions will be provided as a basis for thinking about the "topics" of the movies, as well as the question why these movies became famous and critically acclaimed.
aims:
We will analyze and try to interpret and evaluate a series of recent movies outlined in the lesson plan below.
- Analysis will focus on the narrative structure of the movie plots and its cinematographic realization, i. e. a movie's structural elements (shots > scenes > sequences of scenes > "acts"), and the relation between a movie's plot and its story.
- Interpretation will show how any understanding of a given "text" (here: movie) depends on ("objective") background knowledge and the ("subjective") circumstances of the interpreter.
- Evaluation will reveal that evaluating works of art (here: movies) is a pathway to realize one's own values and expectations, esp. hidden ones.
goals: To introduce famous movies in English
To understand the narratives of these movies together with their cultural background, given an English plot summary
To interpret the movies, i. e. to understand the fundamental "topics" and express them in English
To be able to express opinions about the movies, their topics etc. in English

plan.text:
Movie Analysis:
The Lord Of The Rings: The Fellowship Of The Ring (Peter Jackson, 2001)

Movie Analysis:
Eternal Sunshine Of The Spotless Mind (Michel Gondry, 2004)

Movie Analysis:
The Dark Knight (Christopher Nolan, 2008)

Movie Analysis:
The King's Speech (Tom Hooper, 2010)

Movie Analysis:
12 Years A Slave (Steve McQueen, 2013)

Movie Analysis:
Manchester By The Sea (Kenneth Lonergan, 2016)

textbooks:
Plot summaries and questions guiding the discussion of next week's movie will be provided at the end of each class.
Students who want to read about movies, film analysis and the movie industry could read:
Monaco, James: How To Read A Film. The Art, Technology. Language, History, and Theory of Film and Media. New York: Oxford University Press, 1981 (revised edition).
or:
Sikov, Ed: Film Studies. New York: Columbia University Press, 2020 (second edition).
prereq: Participants must be able to understand and debate in English.
prep: Participants who watch the movies ahead of their introduction in class will be at a clear advantage. Since all the movies introduced belong to the "canon" of world cinematography, many sources pertaining to their analysis and interpretation can be found online.

【NOTES ON CLASS ENROLLMENT】
extra.text: Participants must be able to understand and freely debate in English.

extra.label: 成績評価に関する補足
extra.text:
As their final report, participants will be asked to introduce a (non-Japanese) movie IN ENGLISH, including
- a plot summary,
- a discussion of the "topics" and the cultural background, and
- an evaluation of the movie introduced.

extra.label: オフィスアワーに関する補足
extra.text: Please use Thursday's Office Hour if you can.

extra.label: 教員の実務経験の有無
授業形態
extra.text:
口頭での質疑やディスカッションを含む
その他
Participants must be able to understand and debate IN ENGLISH. Although the class is classified as a "lecture", there will be extended periods of Q&A - IN ENGLISH -, and students' participation will be part (40%) of their evaluation.
